title: Prompts That Actually Work
goal: Write clear prompts that get usable results on the first try.

blocks.html:
A working prompt isn't a magic phrase. It's four plain parts, and when you include them you stop getting vague junk back.

  • Role — who the AI should act as (for example, 'you are a coffee shop owner').
  • Context — the situation ('launching a new oat-milk latte').
  • Task — the exact thing to do ('write 3 Instagram captions').
  • Format — what the answer should look like ('each under 15 words, friendly').

Leave parts out and the AI guesses. Guessing is where slop comes from. Try the quick check, then tick off the parts you already use.

blocks.question: Which of these prompts will actually get you a usable result?

options.explain: Too vague. No role, no real task, no format — you'll get generic filler.

options.text: You are a coffee shop owner launching an oat-milk latte. Write 3 Instagram captions, each under 15 words, friendly tone.
options.explain: Role, context, a clear task, and a format with limits. That's a prompt that returns something you can actually post.
There's nothing to act on — no task, no context. The AI has to invent everything.
A topic, but no role, no count, no length, no tone. The model still has to guess most of it.

items.text: I told it who to act as (a role).
I gave the situation or background.
I stated the exact thing to do.
I said what the answer should look like.

blocks.html: Now build one yourself. Fill the four boxes and hit Build — a strong prompt assembles in front of you. Save the good ones; they land in your library on the dashboard.

blocks.title: My structured prompt
blocks.template: You are a {role}. Context: {context}. Task: {task}. Format: {format}.

fields.label: Context
fields.placeholder: a customer is asking for a refund

fields.label: Task
fields.placeholder: write a polite reply offering a solution

blocks.html: Here's a weak prompt: 'write me some marketing.' Rewrite it in the box so it actually works — give it a role, a clear task, and a format. The checker shows you, rule by rule, what's still missing. No hidden score.

blocks.placeholder: Rewrite 'write me some marketing' into a real prompt…

rules.label: Gives the AI a role ('you are…', 'act as…')
States a clear task (write, draft, list, create…)
Sets a format or limit (bullets, word count, tone…)

lessons.title: Good vs Bad Prompt, Side by Side

blocks.html: Same goal, two prompts. Read both, then reveal why one gets slop and the other gets something you can use straight away.

left.label: Weak
left.text: write something about our new product

right.label: Strong
right.text: You are our marketing lead. Write 3 launch tweets for our new oat-milk latte, each under 20 words, upbeat tone.

why: The strong one names a role, so the AI writes in the right voice.
It gives a specific task and a count (3 tweets), not a vague topic.
It sets limits (under 20 words) and tone (upbeat), so the output is usable as-is.
The weak one makes the AI guess everything — and guessing is where generic slop comes from.
